cases.titulo: Eliminación de la Regla 18.4 en Team Racing
cases.relato_protesta: Y y B solapados en babor hacia la boya de sotavento. Y es interior. Y traslucha a estribor en la zona y orza a B para alejarla de la marca. B es obligada a trasluchar.
cases.output_ideal:
Hechos: Y interior solapado. Y traslucha a estribor en la zona y orza a B .
Norma: Regla 18.2(a)(1) y Apéndice D (D1.1c) según TR CALL J5 .
Rationale: En Team Racing, la Regla 18.4 se borra, por lo que el barco con derecho a espacio de marca no tiene obligación de trasluchar de inmediato y puede orzar al otro .
Decisión: Sin penalización para Y.
cases.query: Y y B solapados en babor hacia la boya de sotavento. Y es interior. Y traslucha a estribor en la zona y orza a B para alejarla de la marca. B es obligada a trasluchar.
cases.answer:
## 1. Síntesis fáctica (hechos encontrados)
Y y B están solapadas en babor hacia la boya de sotavento, con Y como barco interior. Cuando llegan a la zona del obstáculo, Y traslucha a estribor para alejar a B de la marca. Como resultado, B es obligada a trasluchar también.

## 2. Identificación normativa jerarquizada
- **Regla 18.2(a)(2)** — Este artículo establece que el barco claro asta debe dar espacio al barco claro adelante para pasar la boya de manera segura y seamanera.
- **TR CALL A3** — Según TR CALL A3, un barco que está obligado a mantenerse fuera del camino del otro barco debe responder prontamente y de manera seamanera a cualquier cambio de rumbo por parte del barco con derecho de paso.

## 3. Rationale técnico (razonamiento lógico)
Según el relato, Y es el barco interior que llega primero a la zona del obstáculo mientras está claro asta de B. Según Regla 18.2(a)(2), Y debe dar espacio a B para pasar la boya en una manera seamanera. Sin embargo, al trasluchar a estribor y obligar a B a hacer lo mismo, Y no cumple con su obligación de proporcionar el espacio necesario para que B pueda navegar hacia y pasando la boya.

Según TR CALL A3, un barco que está obligado a mantenerse fuera del camino del otro barco debe responder prontamente y de manera seamanera a cualquier cambio de rumbo por parte del barco con derecho de paso. En este caso, B es el barco claro adelante y Y no proporciona el espacio necesario para que B pueda navegar hacia la boya sin interferencia.

## 4. Resolución de la protesta
Dado que Y no cumplió con su obligación de dar espacio a B según Regla 18.2(a)(2) al trasluchar y forzar a B a hacer lo mismo, se debe penalizar a Y por no mantenerse fuera del camino de B.

Decisión: Penalizar a Y.

cases.titulo: Virada completada libre por proa en la zona
cases.relato_protesta: Y vira en la zona de la marca de barlovento por delante de B. Y completa su virada y queda libre por proa, pero B debe caer inmediatamente para evitar chocar con la popa de Y.
cases.output_ideal:
Hechos: Y vira en la zona. B debe caer para evitarla después de que Y completó su virada .
Norma: Regla 12 e interpretación TR CALL E6 .
Rationale: Si Y completa su virada sin obligar a B a maniobrar durante la misma (Regla 13) y sin riesgo de contacto inmediato (Regla 15), B ahora es el barco que viene por popa y debe mantenerse alejado .
Decisión: Sin penalización para Y.
cases.query: Y vira en la zona de la marca de barlovento por delante de B. Y completa su virada y queda libre por proa, pero B debe caer inmediatamente para evitar chocar con la popa de Y.
cases.answer:
## 1. Síntesis fáctica (hechos encontrados)
Y vira en la zona de la marca de barlovento por delante de B, completando su virada y quedándose libre por proa. Como resultado, B debe caer inmediatamente para evitar chocar con la popa de Y.

## 2. Identificación normativa jerarquizada
- **Regla 18.2(a)(2)** — Este artículo establece que una embarcación que llegue a la zona antes de otra, aunque esté claramente asta, debe recibir espacio para pasar el obstáculo en un lado determinado.
- **Regla 14** — Esta regla indica que una embarcación debe evitar cualquier contacto con otra embarcación si es posible hacerlo sin comprometer su posición en la carrera.
- **TR CALL A3** — Este llamado establece que una embarcación que debe mantenerse clara debe responder de manera inmediata y seamanlike a los cambios de curso del barco derecho al paso.

## 3. Rationale técnico (razonamiento lógico)
Según la regla 18.2(a)(2), Y, como la embarcación que llegó primero a la zona de la marca aunque estaba claramente asta, tenía el derecho a recibir espacio para pasar el obstáculo en un lado determinado. Sin embargo, B no pudo mantenerse clara después de que Y completó su virada y quedó libre por proa.

Según TR CALL A3, una embarcación que debe mantenerse clara debe responder de manera inmediata y seamanlike a los cambios de curso del barco derecho al paso. En este caso, B debió haber respondido de manera inmediata para evitar el contacto con la popa de Y.

## 4. Resolución de la protesta
Dado que B no pudo mantenerse clara después de que Y completó su virada y quedó libre por proa, se debe penalizar a B por no haber respondido adecuadamente a los cambios de curso de Y.
Decisión: Penalizar a B.

Position 3: After Y’s first change of course, B still has room to keep clear. B must, promptly and in a seamanlike way, do whatever she can to esta blish sufficient distance between her and Y so that she is keeping clear. If B does this she is protected by rule 16.1. Between 3 and 4: Y continues to change course, and B is no longer able to keep clear. Y breaks rule 16.1. Penalize Y. If Y continues to luff and B continues to respond promptly and in a seamanlike way to each luff, Y will ultimately have to stop luffing when the boats are so close that any further luff from Y will result in B being unable to keep clear. If Y does stop luffing at such a time, and B continues to respond to keep clear, neither boat breaks a rule. A boat that is required to keep clear must promptly take any opportunity to keep clear when a right-of-way boat changes course, but does not normally have to anticipate any change of course by a right-of-way boat.

CASE 1 — A boat that breaks a rule while racing but continues to race may

Rule 60.1, Protests: Right to Protest Rule 60.5(b), Protests: Protest Decisions Rule 60.5(c), Protests: Protest Decisions A boat that breaks a rule while racing but continues to race may protest over a later incident, even though after the race she is penalized for her breach. Facts Boats A, B and C are racing with others. After an incident between A and B, A hails ‘Protest’ and displays a red flag, but B does not take a penalty. Later, B protests a third boat, C, after a second incident. The protest committee hears A’s protest against B and disqualifies B. Question Does the fact that B is disqualified as a result of A’s protest make B’s protest against C invalid? Answer No. When a boat continues to race after an alleged breach of a rule, her rights and obligations under the rules do not change. Consequently, even though A’s protest against B is upheld, the protest committee must hear B’s protest against C and, if B’s protest is valid and the protest committee is satisfied from the evidence that C broke a rule, C must also be penalized (see rules 60.1, 60.5(b) and 60.5(c)). CASE 2 — This case covers a situation involving two boats at a downwind

Rule 12, On the Same Tack, Not Overlapped Rule 14, Avoiding Contact Rule 15, Acquiring Right of Way Rule 18.2(a)(2), Mark-Room: Giving Mark-Room Rule 43.1(c), Exoneration This case covers a situation involving two boats at a downwind mark in which a boat clear astern reaches the zone before a boat clear ahead. In that situation the boat clear ahead is required by rule 18.2(a)(2) to give mark-room to the boat clear astern. Facts A and B were both on port tack, reaching to a mark to be left to starboard. The wind was light. At position 1, when B reached the zone, A was clear ahead of B but four-and-a-half hull lengths from the mark. Between positions 1 and 2 A gybed and headed to the mark, becoming overlapped outside B. Then B gybed and headed to the mark. Between positions 2 and 3, after B had gybed and turned towards the mark, she became clear ahead of A. When B first became clear ahead of A there was about one-half of a hull length of open water between the boats. A few seconds after B became clear ahead, A, who was moving faster, struck B on the transom. There was no damage or injury. B protested A under rule 12 and 18.2(a)(2). A was disqualified and she appealed. Decision At position 1, B was the first of the two to reach the zone, even though she was clear astern of A. Therefore, rule 18.2(a)(2) applied, and it required A, which had not reached the zone at that moment, to give mark-room to B, which was room for B to sail to and past the mark on the required side in a seamanlike way. There are no facts that suggest B sailed outside of the mark-room to which she was entitled after position 1, or that she broke rule 10 while on port tack or rule 15 after gybing and becoming clear ahead of A. If she were to have broken rule 10 or rule 15, she would be exonerated by rule 43.1(b) for those breaches. When A hit B’s transom, she obviously was not keeping clear of B, and so it was proper to disqualify A for breaking rule 12. A also broke rule 18.2(a)(2) when she hit B’s transom because at that moment A was not giving B mark- room. Finally, A also broke rule 14(a) because it was possible for her to bear off slightly and avoid the contact with B. After it became clear that A was not going to keep clear of B, it was not reasonably possible for B to avoid the contact. However, even if B could have avoided the contact but did not do so, she would have been exonerated by rule 43.1(c) because she was the right-of-way boat and the contact did not cause damage or injury. The appeal is dismissed, and the protest committee’s decision is upheld. Finally, A broke rules 12, 14(a) and 18.2(a)(2) and she remains disqualified. USA 1962/87; revised by World Sailing 2025

cases.titulo: Contacto antes de completar la virada tras cruce
cases.relato_protesta: B (estribor) cruza por delante de Y (babor). Después de cruzar, B orza y vira a babor. Durante la virada, B choca con Y antes de llegar a rumbo de ceñida.
cases.output_ideal:
Hechos: B cruza a Y. B vira y choca con Y antes de completar la virada .
Norma: Regla 13 según TR CALL D4 .
Rationale: B perdió su derecho de paso al virar. Mientras un barco vira, debe mantenerse alejado de los demás .
Decisión: Penalizar a B.
cases.query: B (estribor) cruza por delante de Y (babor). Después de cruzar, B orza y vira a babor. Durante la virada, B choca con Y antes de llegar a rumbo de ceñida.
cases.answer:
## 1. Síntesis fáctica (hechos encontrados)
B (estribor) cruza por delante de Y (babor). Después de cruzarse, B orza y vira hacia babor. Durante la virada, B choca con Y antes de llegar a rumbo de ceñida.

## 2. Identificación normativa jerarquizada
- **Regla 14** — [B debe evitar cualquier contacto que pueda causar daño o lesiones]
- **TR CALL A3** — [Y y B están en una situación donde Y tiene la obligación de cambiar su rumbo para permitir a B mantenerse claramente fuera. Si Y continúa ladeándose, Y viola Regla 16.1]
- **Regla 16.1** — [B debe hacer todo lo posible y de manera seamanlike para evitar el contacto]

## 3. Rationale técnico (razonamiento lógico)
Según la descripción del incidente, B cruza por delante de Y y luego orza hacia babor. Durante esta maniobra, B choca con Y antes de completar su virada a ceñida. Según Regla 14, una vez que el contacto ocurre, es responsabilidad de la embarcación que no tiene derecho de vía (en este caso, B) evitar cualquier contacto que pueda causar daño o lesiones.

Según TR CALL A3, cuando dos barcos están en una situación donde uno debe mantenerse claramente fuera del otro, el barco que está obligado a cambiar su rumbo para permitir al otro mantenerse claramente fuera (en este caso, B) debe hacer todo lo posible y de manera seamanlike para evitar cualquier contacto. En este incidente, B no logró mantenerse claramente fuera de Y durante la maniobra de virar hacia babor.

Además, según Regla 16.1, una vez que un barco cambia su rumbo (en este caso, Y), el otro barco debe hacer todo lo posible y de manera seamanlike para mantenerse claramente fuera del primero. En este incidente, B no logró evitar el contacto con Y durante la maniobra.

## 4. Resolución de la protesta
Dado que B choca con Y antes de completar su virada a ceñida y no logra mantenerse claramente fuera de Y según Regla 16.1, se debe penalizar a B por no cumplir con sus obligaciones bajo las reglas.

Decisión: Penalizar a B.

Rule 13 While Tacking Rule 17 On the Same Tack; Proper Course Question On a beat to windward, an overlap is established while one boat is tacking or shortly thereafter. Under which of the following conditions is B, the leeward boat, permitted to sail above her proper course?

Answer Figure 1: Overlap established from clear astern - B does not acquire luffing rights. When Y passes head to wind in position 2 the boats are on the same tack, but not overlapped. After B establishes an overlap from clear astern and she will break rule 17 if she sails above her proper course during this overlap, and while the boats remain within two hull lengths of each other, unless in doing so she promptly sails astern of Y. Figure 2: Overlap established 'instantaneously'. At position 2, when B passes through head to wind, both boats are on the same tack and overlapped. Rule 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped Definitions Keep Clear Question B closes on Y from windward. When is B no longer keeping clear of Y? cases.titulo: Táctica de frenado en la marca (The Trap)
cases.relato_protesta: Tres barcos (B, X, Y) se acercan a una boya por babor. X es el barco más a sotavento y líder. Y y B están solapados por fuera. X frena bruscamente para "atrapar" a B y permitir que su compañero Y pase por el interior. B choca con X.
cases.output_ideal:
Hechos: X frena en la marca para atrapar a B. B choca con X .
Norma: Regla 11 y 18.2 según TR CALL J9 .
Rationale: X es el barco con derecho de paso y espacio de marca. B (barlovento) debe mantenerse alejada de X independientemente de la táctica de equipo de X .
Decisión: Penalizar a X.
cases.query: Tres barcos (B, X, Y) se acercan a una boya por babor. X es el barco más a sotavento y líder. Y y B están solapados por fuera. X frena bruscamente para "atrapar" a B y permitir que su compañero Y pase por el interior. B choca con X.
cases.answer:
## 1. Síntesis fáctica (hechos encontrados)
Tres barcos (B, X, Y) se acercan a una boya por babor. X es el barco más a sotavento y líder. Y y B están solapados por fuera. X frena bruscamente para "atrapar" a B y permitir que su compañero Y pase por el interior. Como resultado, B choca con X.

## 2. Identificación normativa jerarquizada
- **Regla 14** — [X debe evitar cualquier contacto con otros barcos mientras navega]
- **Regla 15** — [B adquiere derecho de vía al cambiar a una posición más adelantada que X]
- **TR CALL A3** — [situaciones similares donde un barco debe mantenerse claro ante otro]
- **Case 2** — [aplica cuando un barco no da suficiente espacio a otro en la zona del marcador]

## 3. Rationale técnico (razonamiento lógico)
Según el relato, X es el líder y está más a sotavento que B. Y y B están solapados por fuera. Cuando X frena bruscamente para permitir que su compañero Y pase por dentro, B choca con X. Según la Regla 15, B adquiere derecho de vía al cambiar a una posición más adelantada que X. Además, según TR CALL A3, un barco que debe mantenerse claro ante otro (en este caso B) debe hacerlo de manera pronta y marinera.

X no cumplió con la Regla 14 ya que permitió el contacto con B al frenar bruscamente. Según Case 2, si un barco no da suficiente espacio a otro en la zona del marcador (en este caso X), se considera una violación de las reglas.

## 4. Resolución de la protesta
Decisión: Penalizar a X.
La decisión es penalizar a X ya que permitió el contacto con B al frenar bruscamente, incumpliendo así la Regla 14 y no dando suficiente espacio a B conforme a la Regla 15. Además, según Case 2, X debe dar mar-room (espacio para pasar el marcador) a B que es el barco más cercano al marcador en ese momento. Por lo tanto, X incumple las reglas y se penaliza a X.

There are no facts that suggest B sailed outside of the mark-room to which she was entitled after position 1, or that she broke rule 10 while on port tack or rule 15 after gybing and becoming clear ahead of A. If she were to have broken rule 10 or rule 15, she would be exonerated by rule 43.1(b) for those breaches. When A hit B’s transom, she obviously was not keeping clear of B, and so it was proper to disqualify A for breaking rule 12. A also broke rule 18.2(a)(2) when she hit B’s transom because at that moment A was not giving B mark- room. Finally, A also broke rule 14(a) because it was possible for her to bear off slightly and avoid the contact with B. After it became clear that A was not going to keep clear of B, it was not reasonably possible for B to avoid the contact. However, even if B could have avoided the contact but did not do so, she would have been exonerated by rule 43.1(c) because she was the right-of-way boat and the contact did not cause damage or injury. The appeal is dismissed, and the protest committee’s decision is upheld. Finally, A broke rules 12, 14(a) and 18.2(a)(2) and she remains disqualified. USA 1962/87; revised by World Sailing 2025

cases.relato_protesta: Y navega por la amura de babor. B navega por la amura de estribor. Y tiene que virar para evitar a B, pero durante la maniobra el tripulante de B empuja la botavara hacia afuera deliberadamente para tocar a Y.

expected.decision_text: Penalizar a B. Exonerar a Y.

cases.output_ideal:
Hechos: Y en babor, B en estribor. Y vira para evitar a B. B empuja la botavara para causar contacto .
Norma: Regla 10 y D2.3(f) según TR CALL L1 .
Rationale: Es un incumplimiento de la deportividad provocar contacto deliberadamente cuando el otro barco estaba manteniéndose alejado .

cases.query: Y navega por la amura de babor. B navega por la amura de estribor. Y tiene que virar para evitar a B, pero durante la maniobra el tripulante de B empuja la botavara hacia afuera deliberadamente para tocar a Y.
cases.answer:
## 1. Síntesis fáctica (hechos encontrados)
Y navega por la amura de babor, mientras que B navega por la amura de estribor. Durante su maniobra para evitar a Y, el tripulante de B empuja deliberadamente la botavara hacia afuera con el objetivo de tocar a Y.

## 2. Identificación normativa jerarquizada
- **Regla 14** — [Y no pudo evitar el contacto ya que B actuó intencionalmente para causarlo, lo cual es una violación directa de la regla 14]
- **TR CALL A3** — [Según TR CALL A3, si una embarcación está obligada a mantenerse clara y responde adecuadamente al cambio de curso de otra embarcación, no se considera que haya cometido una falta. Sin embargo, en este caso, B actuó deliberadamente para causar el contacto]
- **Regla 16.1** — [B no pudo cumplir con la regla 16.1 ya que su acción fue intencional y no un cambio de curso necesario para mantenerse clara]

## 3. Rationale técnico (razonamiento lógico)
Según TR CALL A3, una embarcación que está obligada a mantenerse clara debe responder adecuadamente al cambio de curso de otra embarcación. Sin embargo, en este caso, B actuó deliberadamente para causar el contacto con Y durante la maniobra de evasión, lo cual es una violación directa de la regla 14 que prohíbe las acciones intencionadas para causar daño o contacto.

Además, según Regla 16.1, B no pudo cumplir con su obligación de mantenerse clara ya que su acción fue deliberada y no un cambio de curso necesario para evitar el contacto. La regla 14 establece que una embarcación debe evitar cualquier acción intencional que cause daño o contacto.

## 4. Resolución de la protesta
Dado que B actuó deliberadamente para causar el contacto con Y, se considera que ha violado la Regla 14 y no pudo cumplir con su obligación de mantenerse clara según la Regla 16.1.
Decisión: Penalizar a B.

TR CALL A1 — 13 While Tacking; 17 On the Same Tack; Proper Course

Rule 13 While Tacking Rule 17 On the Same Tack; Proper Course Question On a beat to windward, an overlap is established while one boat is tacking or shortly thereafter. Under which of the following conditions is B, the leeward boat, permitted to sail above her proper course?

Answer Figure 1: Overlap established from clear astern - B does not acquire luffing rights. When Y passes head to wind in position 2 the boats are on the same tack, but not overlapped. After B establishes an overlap from clear astern and she will break rule 17 if she sails above her proper course during this overlap, and while the boats remain within two hull lengths of each other, unless in doing so she promptly sails astern of Y. Figure 2: Overlap established 'instantaneously'. At position 2, when B passes through head to wind, both boats are on the same tack and overlapped. As neither boat established this overlap from clear astern, the overlap is said to be 'instantaneous'. Rule 17 does not apply and B may sail above her proper course. A tacking boat changes tack the moment she passes head to wind. If another boat becomes overlapped to leeward of her from clear astern during the tacking boat passes head to wind, rule 17 applies and the leeward boat may not sail above her proper course.

TR CALL A2 — 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped

Rule 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped Definitions Keep Clear Question B closes on Y from windward. When is B no longer keeping clear of Y? Answer B is keeping clear of Y when Y, the right-of-way boat, can sail her course with no need to take avoiding action and also, because they are overlapped, change course in both directions without immediately making contact. At positions 1 & 2: B is keeping clear. At position 3: if Y luffs, there will be contact but not immediately, so B is still keeping clear. At position 4: Y cannot change course to windward without immediately making contact with B. B is no longer keeping clear, and would be penalized under rule 11 if Y were to protest, whether or not the boats make contact. At any moment, a boat’s course (as that term is used in Part 2 and the Definitions) means the direction in which her bow is pointing. It does not mean her proper course, nor the course she could be expected to be sailing shortly. There does not need to be contact for a boat to fail to keep clear.

retrieved.header: [TR CALL A3 — Sección: SECTION A – DEFINITIONS AND FUNDAMENTALS — Reglas: 11, 16.1 — pp. 10-11 — Call-Book-TR (call_book_calls.csv)]
retrieved.text:
TR CALL A3 — 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped; 16.1 Changing Course

Rule 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped Rule 16.1 Changing Course Definitions Keep Clear Question Y and B are overlapped, sailing parallel courses, with about ½ boat’s width between them. Y has no proper course restriction. Just before position 3 Y luffs s lowly and B promptly responds. Y continues to luff and there is no longer any means of B avoiding Y. B tries to keep clear by luffing and there is contact. Y protests. What should the call be?

Answer Positions 1 and 2: B is keeping clear because she is su fficiently far from Y that Y is able to change course in both directions without immediately making contact. Position 3: After Y’s first change of course, B still has room to keep clear. B must, promptly and in a seamanlike way, do whatever she can to esta blish sufficient distance between her and Y so that she is keeping clear. If B does this she is protected by rule 16.1. Between 3 and 4: Y continues to change course, and B is no longer able to keep clear. Y breaks rule 16.1. Penalize Y. If Y continues to luff and B continues to respond promptly and in a seamanlike way to each luff, Y will ultimately have to stop luffing when the boats are so close that any further luff from Y will result in B being unable to keep clear. If Y does stop luffing at such a time, and B continues to respond to keep clear, neither boat breaks a rule. A boat that is required to keep clear must promptly take any opportunity to keep clear when a right-of-way boat changes course, but does not normally have to anticipate any change of course by a right-of-way boat.

CASE 1 — A boat that breaks a rule while racing but continues to race may

Rule 60.1, Protests: Right to Protest Rule 60.5(b), Protests: Protest Decisions Rule 60.5(c), Protests: Protest Decisions A boat that breaks a rule while racing but continues to race may protest over a later incident, even though after the race she is penalized for her breach. Facts Boats A, B and C are racing with others. After an incident between A and B, A hails ‘Protest’ and displays a red flag, but B does not take a penalty. Later, B protests a third boat, C, after a second incident. The protest committee hears A’s protest against B and disqualifies B. Answer No. When a boat continues to race after an alleged breach of a rule, her rights and obligations under the rules do not change. Consequently, even though A’s protest against B is upheld, the protest committee must hear B’s protest against C and, if B’s protest is valid and the protest committee is satisfied from the evidence that C broke a rule, C must also be penalized (see rules 60.1, 60.5(b) and 60.5(c)). cases.titulo: Trasluchada para "apagar" la restricción de la Regla 17
cases.relato_protesta: B y Y están en el tramo de popa, solapados en la misma amura. B es sotavento y tiene restricción de rumbo debido (Regla 17). B traslucha a la amura de babor y vuelve a trasluchar a estribor de inmediato para orzar a Y.
cases.output_ideal:
Hechos: B traslucha dos veces. B orza a Y tras las trasluchadas .
Norma: Regla 17 según TR CALL G3 .
Rationale: Si la trasluchada es real (la vela se llena en la nueva amura), el solapamiento original se rompe y con él la restricción de la Regla 17 .
Decisión: Sin penalización para B.
cases.query: B y Y están en el tramo de popa, solapados en la misma amura. B es sotavento y tiene restricción de rumbo debido (Regla 17). B traslucha a la amura de babor y vuelve a trasluchar a estribor de inmediato para orzar a Y.
cases.answer:
## 1. Síntesis fáctica (hechos encontrados)
B y Y están en el tramo de popa, solapados en la misma amura. B es sotavento y tiene restricción de rumbo debido a que está bajo la influencia de Regla 17. B traslucha a la amura de babor y vuelve a trasluchar inmediatamente a estribor para orzar a Y.

## 2. Identificación normativa jerarquizada
- **Regla 16.1** — Según esta regla, una vez que un barco está obligado a mantenerse fuera del camino de otro barco (regla 15), debe hacerlo en forma inmediata y seamanlike.
- **TR CALL A3** — Esta llamada establece que si el barco sotavento (B) responde prontamente y de manera seamanlike a cada cambio de rumbo del barco viento arriba (Y), no rompe la regla. Sin embargo, si B continúa respondiendo mientras Y luffea sin cesar hasta que ya no puede mantenerse fuera del camino de Y, entonces B no rompe la regla.
- **Regla 17** — Esta regla establece las restricciones para el barco sotavento (B) en cuanto a los cambios de rumbo.

## 3. Rationale técnico (razonamiento lógico)
Según Regla 16.1, una vez que B se encuentra obligado a mantenerse fuera del camino de Y debido a la restricción de rumbo bajo Regla 17, debe hacerlo de manera inmediata y seamanlike. En este caso, B traslucha a babor y luego vuelve a trasluchar a estribor para orzar a Y. Esta serie de cambios de rumbo no parece ser una respuesta pronta y seamanlike a la situación.

Según TR CALL A3, si el barco sotavento (B) responde prontamente y de manera seamanlike a cada cambio de rumbo del barco viento arriba (Y), entonces B no rompe la regla. Sin embargo, en este caso, los cambios de rumbo de B parecen ser excesivos y no necesarios para mantenerse fuera del camino de Y.

## 4. Resolución de la protesta
Dado que B realizó una serie de trasluches inmediatos sin un motivo claro para mantenerse fuera del camino de Y, se considera que B rompió Regla 16.1 al no responder prontamente y de manera seamanlike a la situación.

Decisión: Penalizar a B.

cases.titulo: Interferencia de un barco que ya terminó la regata
cases.relato_protesta: Y ha terminado la regata y está navegando de regreso. En su camino, pasa cerca de B que aún está compitiendo, obligando a B a virar para evitarla.
cases.output_ideal:
Hechos: Y ha terminado. B sigue compitiendo. Y obliga a B a maniobrar .
Norma: Regla D1.1(e) según TR CALL K1 .
Rationale: Un barco que ha terminado no debe interferir con un barco que aún está compitiendo .
Decisión: Penalizar a Y.
cases.query: Y ha terminado la regata y está navegando de regreso. En su camino, pasa cerca de B que aún está compitiendo, obligando a B a virar para evitarla.
cases.answer:
## 1. Síntesis fáctica (hechos encontrados)
Y ha terminado la regata y está navegando de regreso en dirección opuesta a B, que aún está compitiendo. En su camino, Y pasa cerca de B, obligándola a virar para evitar el contacto.

## 2. Identificación normativa jerarquizada
- **Regla 14** — Según la Regla 14, una embarcación debe evitar cualquier acción que pueda causar un contacto con otra embarcación.
- **TR CALL A3** — Según TR CALL A3, si B responde prontamente y de manera marinera a los cambios de curso de Y para mantenerse clara, no se considera que haya violado la regla. Sin embargo, en este caso, Y es la embarcación que está causando el problema al pasar cerca de B.
- **Regla 18.2(a)(2)** — Según Regla 18.2(a)(2), una embarcación que llega a un obstáculo o marco desde atrás debe ser dada espacio para navegar alrededor del mismo en forma marinera.

## 3. Rationale técnico (razonamiento lógico)
Según la regla 14, Y ha causado un contacto potencial con B al pasar cerca de ella mientras navegaba en dirección opuesta a la competencia. Esto implica que Y no está evitando el contacto y está violando la regla.

Además, según TR CALL A3, si una embarcación debe mantenerse clara ante otra, esta debe responder prontamente y de manera marinera para evitar cualquier contacto. En este caso, B ha tenido que virar para evitar a Y, lo cual indica que Y no está dando espacio adecuado.

Finalmente, aunque la regla 18.2(a)(2) se refiere específicamente a situaciones en las proximidades de un obstáculo o marco, el espíritu general de esta regla es aplicable aquí: una embarcación que llega desde atrás debe dar espacio para navegar alrededor del obstáculo. En este caso, Y no está dando ese espacio a B.

## 4. Resolución de la protesta
Dado que Y ha causado un contacto potencial con B y no ha dado el espacio adecuado para evitarlo, se debe penalizar a Y por violar las reglas pertinentes.
Decisión: Penalizar a Y.

TR CALL A1 — 13 While Tacking; 17 On the Same Tack; Proper Course

Rule 13 While Tacking Rule 17 On the Same Tack; Proper Course Question On a beat to windward, an overlap is established while one boat is tacking or shortly thereafter. Under which of the following conditions is B, the leeward boat, permitted to sail above her proper course?

Answer Figure 1: Overlap established from clear astern - B does not acquire luffing rights. When Y passes head to wind in position 2 the boats are on the same tack, but not overlapped. After B establishes an overlap from clear astern and she will break rule 17 if she sails above her proper course during this overlap, and while the boats remain within two hull lengths of each other, unless in doing so she promptly sails astern of Y. Figure 2: Overlap established 'instantaneously'. At position 2, when B passes through head to wind, both boats are on the same tack and overlapped. As neither boat established this overlap from clear astern, the overlap is said to be 'instantaneous'. Rule 17 does not apply and B may sail above her proper course. A tacking boat changes tack the moment she passes head to wind. If another boat becomes overlapped to leeward of her from clear astern during the tacking boat passes head to wind, rule 17 applies and the leeward boat may not sail above her proper course.

retrieved.header: [TR CALL A2 — Sección: SECTION A – DEFINITIONS AND FUNDAMENTALS — Reglas: 11 — pp. 9-10 — Call-Book-TR (call_book_calls.csv)]
retrieved.text:
TR CALL A2 — 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped

Rule 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped Definitions Keep Clear Question B closes on Y from windward. When is B no longer keeping clear of Y? Answer B is keeping clear of Y when Y, the right-of-way boat, can sail her course with no need to take avoiding action and also, because they are overlapped, change course in both directions without immediately making contact. At positions 1 & 2: B is keeping clear. At position 3: if Y luffs, there will be contact but not immediately, so B is still keeping clear. At position 4: Y cannot change course to windward without immediately making contact with B. B is no longer keeping clear, and would be penalized under rule 11 if Y were to protest, whether or not the boats make contact. At any moment, a boat’s course (as that term is used in Part 2 and the Definitions) means the direction in which her bow is pointing. It does not mean her proper course, nor the course she could be expected to be sailing shortly. There does not need to be contact for a boat to fail to keep clear.

retrieved.header: [TR CALL A3 — Sección: SECTION A – DEFINITIONS AND FUNDAMENTALS — Reglas: 11, 16.1 — pp. 10-11 — Call-Book-TR (call_book_calls.csv)]
retrieved.text:
TR CALL A3 — 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped; 16.1 Changing Course

Rule 11 On the Same Tack, Overlapped Rule 16.1 Changing Course Definitions Keep Clear Question Y and B are overlapped, sailing parallel courses, with about ½ boat’s width between them. Y has no proper course restriction. Just before position 3 Y luffs s lowly and B promptly responds. Y continues to luff and there is no longer any means of B avoiding Y. B tries to keep clear by luffing and there is contact. Y protests. What should the call be?

Answer Positions 1 and 2: B is keeping clear because she is su fficiently far from Y that Y is able to change course in both directions without immediately making contact. Position 3: After Y’s first change of course, B still has room to keep clear. B must, promptly and in a seamanlike way, do whatever she can to esta blish sufficient distance between her and Y so that she is keeping clear. If B does this she is protected by rule 16.1. Between 3 and 4: Y continues to change course, and B is no longer able to keep clear. Y breaks rule 16.1. Penalize Y. If Y continues to luff and B continues to respond promptly and in a seamanlike way to each luff, Y will ultimately have to stop luffing when the boats are so close that any further luff from Y will result in B being unable to keep clear. If Y does stop luffing at such a time, and B continues to respond to keep clear, neither boat breaks a rule. A boat that is required to keep clear must promptly take any opportunity to keep clear when a right-of-way boat changes course, but does not normally have to anticipate any change of course by a right-of-way boat.

CASE 1 — A boat that breaks a rule while racing but continues to race may

Rule 60.1, Protests: Right to Protest Rule 60.5(b), Protests: Protest Decisions Rule 60.5(c), Protests: Protest Decisions A boat that breaks a rule while racing but continues to race may protest over a later incident, even though after the race she is penalized for her breach. Facts Boats A, B and C are racing with others. After an incident between A and B, A hails ‘Protest’ and displays a red flag, but B does not take a penalty. Later, B protests a third boat, C, after a second incident. The protest committee hears A’s protest against B and disqualifies B. Question Does the fact that B is disqualified as a result of A’s protest make B’s protest against C invalid? Answer No. When a boat continues to race after an alleged breach of a rule, her rights and obligations under the rules do not change. Consequently, even though A’s protest against B is upheld, the protest committee must hear B’s protest against C and, if B’s protest is valid and the protest committee is satisfied from the evidence that C broke a rule, C must also be penalized (see rules 60.1, 60.5(b) and 60.5(c)). GBR 1962/25

retrieved.header: [CASE 2 — Sección: 60.5(c), Protests: Protest Decisions — Reglas: 12, 14, 15, 18.2(a)(2), 43.1(c) — pp. 76-78 — Case-Book-WS (case_book_cases.csv)]
retrieved.text:
CASE 2 — This case covers a situation involving two boats at a downwind

Rule 12, On the Same Tack, Not Overlapped Rule 14, Avoiding Contact Rule 15, Acquiring Right of Way Rule 18.2(a)(2), Mark-Room: Giving Mark-Room Rule 43.1(c), Exoneration This case covers a situation involving two boats at a downwind mark in which a boat clear astern reaches the zone before a boat clear ahead. In that situation the boat clear ahead is required by rule 18.2(a)(2) to give mark-room to the boat clear astern. Facts A and B were both on port tack, reaching to a mark to be left to starboard. The wind was light. At position 1, when B reached the zone, A was clear ahead of B but four-and-a-half hull lengths from the mark. Between positions 1 and 2 A gybed and headed to the mark, becoming overlapped outside B. Then B gybed and headed to the mark. Between positions 2 and 3, after B had gybed and turned towards the mark, she became clear ahead of A. When B first became clear ahead of A there was about one-half of a hull length of open water between the boats. A few seconds after B became clear ahead, A, who was moving faster, struck B on the transom. There was no damage or injury. B protested A under rule 12 and 18.2(a)(2). A was disqualified and she appealed. Decision At position 1, B was the first of the two to reach the zone, even though she was clear astern of A. Therefore, rule 18.2(a)(2) applied, and it required A, which had not reached the zone at that moment, to give mark-room to B, which was room for B to sail to and past the mark on the required side in a seamanlike way. There are no facts that suggest B sailed outside of the mark-room to which she was entitled after position 1, or that she broke rule 10 while on port tack or rule 15 after gybing and becoming clear ahead of A. If she were to have broken rule 10 or rule 15, she would be exonerated by rule 43.1(b) for those breaches. When A hit B’s transom, she obviously was not keeping clear of B, and so it was proper to disqualify A for breaking rule 12. A also broke rule 18.2(a)(2) when she hit B’s transom because at that moment A was not giving B mark- room. Finally, A also broke rule 14(a) because it was possible for her to bear off slightly and avoid the contact with B. After it became clear that A was not going to keep clear of B, it was not reasonably possible for B to avoid the contact. However, even if B could have avoided the contact but did not do so, she would have been exonerated by rule 43.1(c) because she was the right-of-way boat and the contact did not cause damage or injury. The appeal is dismissed, and the protest committee’s decision is upheld. Finally, A broke rules 12, 14(a) and 18.2(a)(2) and she remains disqualified. USA 1962/87; revised by World Sailing 2025

cases.titulo: Virada en proa obligando a maniobra evitativa inmediata
cases.relato_protesta: B (estribor) y Y (babor) en ceñida. B está claramente por delante y Y pasaría por su popa sin problemas. B decide virar justo en la proa de Y, obligando a Y a virar de inmediato para evitar el choque.
cases.output_ideal:
Hechos: B vira frente a Y. Y debe virar para evitar contacto .
Norma: Regla 13 y 15 según TR CALL D3 .
Rationale: B al virar adquiere derecho de paso pero debe dar espacio inicialmente a Y para que se mantenga alejada de forma marinera .
Decisión: Penalizar a B.
cases.query: B (estribor) y Y (babor) en ceñida. B está claramente por delante y Y pasaría por su popa sin problemas. B decide virar justo en la proa de Y, obligando a Y a virar de inmediato para evitar el choque.
cases.answer:
## 1. Síntesis fáctica (hechos encontrados)
B (estribor) y Y (babor) navegaban en ceñida, con B claramente por delante de Y. En ese momento, B decide virar justo frente a la proa de Y, lo que obliga a Y a realizar una maniobra brusca para evitar el choque inminente.

## 2. Identificación normativa jerarquizada
- **Regla 13** — [B se convirtió en la embarcación con derecho de vía al virar y tomar una posición más adelantada, según Regla 13]
- **Regla 14** — [Y estaba obligado a evitar el contacto inminente, pero no tenía tiempo suficiente para hacerlo, según Regla 14]
- **Regla 15** — [B debía dar a Y espacio y tiempo para mantenerse fuera del camino de B al virar, según Regla 15]
- **Case 27** — [Según CASE 27, una embarcación no debe anticipar que otra va a romper las reglas]

## 3. Rationale técnico (razonamiento lógico)
B decidió realizar un viraje justo frente a Y, lo cual es inesperado y no permite a Y reaccionar adecuadamente para evitar el choque. Según Regla 15, cuando una embarcación adquiere derecho de vía como resultado de su propia acción (en este caso, B al virar), la otra embarcación (Y) tiene derecho a tiempo y espacio suficiente para mantenerse fuera del camino. Además, según CASE 27, Y no estaba obligada a anticipar que B iba a romper las reglas al realizar un viraje inesperado.

## 4. Resolución de la protesta
B debe ser penalizada por no dar a Y el tiempo y espacio suficiente para mantenerse fuera del camino al realizar su maniobra brusca.
Decisión: Penalizar a B.

retrieved.text:
TR CALL C3 — 21.1 Starting Errors; 23.2 Interfering with Another Boat

Rule 21.1 Starting Errors Rule 23.2 Interfering with Another Boat Question After the start, B is sailing on port tack towards the pre -start side of the starting line without having started. Y has started correctly and bears away below her proper course onto a collision course with B. B gybes and keeps clear of Y. B protests. What should the call be? Answer Penalize Y. At position 1, B is keeping clear of Y as required by rule 21.1. When Y bears away after position 1 she breaks rule 23.2 as she is no longer sailing her proper course and she interferes with B, a boat subject to rule 21.1. After the starting signal, if a boat is sailing towards the pre -start side of the line, the umpires will use the principle of the ‘last point of certainty’ and decide the boat has not started, unless they are certain that she has started.

TR CALL L5 — 21.2 Taking a Penalty; 44.2 One-Turn and Two-Turns Penalties

Rule 21.2 Taking a Penalty Rule 44.2 One-Turn and Two-Turns Penalties Rule D2.3 Umpire-Initiated Decisions Question 1 How will the umpires decide that a boat has taken a penalty correctly under rule 44.2? Answer 1 A boat takes a penalty correctly when, as soon as possible after an incident or after being penalized by the umpires, she first sails well clear and then makes the required number of turns. The answers below clarify these requirements. Question 2

A and B are opponents of Y. Rule 17 does not apply to Y who has been penalized. A has to alter course and slow down to avoid Y and protests. What should the call be?

Answer 2 Although Y breaks no rule of Part 2, she fails to sail clear as soon as possible. In position 1 she is able to slow down, fall astern of both B and A and then sail clear. Penalize Y under rule D2.3(e). Question 3 When is a boat subject to rule 21.2? Answer 3 Rule 21.2 begins to apply when a boat is clearly turning in order to take a penalty. It ceases to apply when the boat completes the last required tack or gybe or when she stops taking her penalty before it is complete.

SECTION L – OTHER RULES Question 4 When may the umpires take action against a boat that has failed to take a penalty correctly? Answer 4 The umpires should penalize a boat that fails to take a penalty correctly as follows (a) under rule D2.3(e) when the boat has clearly indicated that she will take a penalty and breaks rule D1.3(b); (b) under rule D2.3(e) when the penalty was signalled by an umpire and the boat breaks rule D2.5. In both cases, the failure may be a failure to get well clear as soon as possible, or a failure to take the penalty correctly in accordance with rule 44.2. A right-of-way boat that is sailing to get clear retains right of way until rule 21.2 starts to apply to her. A boat that interferes with another boat while sailing clear, when she has a clear alternative, does not sail clear as soon as possible.

[There are no Calls L6 and L7]

TR CALL M10 — D1.1(d) Changes to the Rules of Part 2; D2.3 Umpire-Initiated Decisions

Rule D1.1(d) Changes to the Rules of Part 2 Rule D2.3 Umpire-Initiated Decisions Question 1 The sailing instructions state that rule D1.1(d) applies requiring clear and repeated arm signals, in addition to hails. If there is an arm signal but no hail from Y, what action should the umpires take a) if B protests? b) on their own initiative? Answer 1 a) When the umpires are certain that no hail for room to tack was made, then the signal for room to tack has not been made. (The umpires may be certain there was no hail if they have had no difficulty hearing other calls from a similar position.) For the purpose of the rules of Part 2 they should act as if there has been no signal. b) The umpires may decide that Y has broken rule D2.3(f) and penalize her. They could also, in an extreme or repeated case, display a black and white flag to report the incident to the protest committee. Question 2 If there is a hail but no arm signal, is the answer the same? Answer 2 Yes.

CASE 20 — When it is possible that a boat is in danger, another boat that

Rule 1.1, Safety: Helping Those in Danger Rule 61.4(b)(4), Redress: Redress Decisions When it is possible that a boat is in danger, another boat that gives help is entitled to redress, even if her help was not asked for or if it is later found that there was no danger. Facts Dinghy A capsized during a race and seeing this dinghy B sailed over to her and offered help. A accepted help and B came alongside, taking the crew of two aboard. Then all hands worked for several minutes to right A, whose mast was stuck in the mud. Upon reaching shore, B requested redress under rule 61.4(b) (4). The protest committee considered several factors in its decision. First, A’s helmsman was a highly experienced sailor. Secondly, the wind was light, and the tide was rising and would shortly have lifted the mast free. Thirdly, she did not ask for help; it was offered. Therefore, since neither boat nor crew was in danger, redress was refused. B appealed, stating that rule 1.1 does not place any onus on a boat giving help to decide, or to defend, a decision that danger was involved. Decision B’s appeal is upheld. A boat in a position to help another that may be in danger is required by rule 1.1 to do so. It is not relevant that a protest committee later decides that there was, in fact, no danger or that help was not requested. B is entitled to redress. The protest committee is directed to reopen the hearing and to grant appropriate redress following the requirements and advice given in rules 61.4(c), 61.4(d) and A9. GBR 1968/14

retrieved.text:
CASE 27 — A boat is not required to anticipate that another boat will break

Rule 2, Fair Sailing Rule 13, While Tacking Rule 14, Avoiding Contact Rule 15, Acquiring Right of Way Rule 43, Exoneration A boat is not required to anticipate that another boat will break a rule. When a boat acquires right of way as a result of her own actions, the other boat is entitled to room to keep clear. Facts When AS reached the zone, she was clear ahead of BP. Between position 1 and 2, AS, a hull length to leeward and a hull length ahead of BP, tacked as soon as she reached the starboard-tack lay line. Almost immediately she was hit and damaged by BP travelling at about ten knots. The protest committee disqualified AS for breaking rule 15. It also disqualified BP under rule 2, pointing out that she knew AS was going to tack but did nothing to avoid a collision. BP appealed, asserting that she was not obligated to anticipate an illegal tack. Decision After AS reached the zone, BP was required by rule 12 to keep clear of her and by rule 18.2(a)(2) to give her mark-room. Both these obligations ended when AS passed head to wind (see rules 18.1(a)(1) and 18.2(b)). When AS passed head to wind, BP became the right-of-way boat under rule 13 and held right of way until AS assumed a close-hauled course on starboard tack. At that moment AS, having just acquired right of way under rule 10, was required by rule 15 to give BP room to keep clear. The collision occurred almost immediately after AS assumed a close-hauled course on starboard tack. Therefore, BP needed to take avoiding action before AS had borne away to a close-hauled course. At that time BP had right of way under rule 13, and so AS broke rule 13. It is a principle of the right-of-way rules, as stated in rule 15, that a boat that becomes obligated to keep clear by an action of another boat is entitled to sufficient time and space to respond. When AS acquired right of way under rule 10, she did not give BP room to keep clear and broke rule 15. Finally, AS broke rule 14(a) because she could have avoided the contact by turning back onto port tack after she passed head to wind. BP took no action to avoid the collision, but what could she have done? The second sentence of rule 14 states that a right-of-way boat need not act to avoid contact until it is clear that the other boat is not keeping clear . Given her speed and the distance involved after it became clear that AS was not keeping clear, BP had perhaps one to two seconds to decide what to do and then do it. While it was obvious that AS would eventually tack to round the mark, no rule required BP to anticipate that AS would break a rule. BP did break rule 10, but she was exonerated for that breach by either rule 43.1(a) or rule 43.1(b). BP did not break rule 14 because it was not reasonably possible for her to have avoided the collision after AS broke rule 13. BP did not violate any principle of sportsmanship or fair play and, therefore, did not break rule 2. BP’s appeal is upheld. She is to be reinstated. AS remains disqualified. USA 1971/140
